mappings.rationaleAI: Jung's concepts of the collective unconscious, archetypes (Shadow, Anima/Animus, Self), psychological types (introversion/extraversion), and individuation directly address psychoanalytic theories of personality and the subjective aspect of personality including awareness of self.

Hysteria covers the conversion of psychological distress into physical symptoms (conversion disorder), Charcot's neurological demonstrations, and Freud and Breuer's psychoanalytic reinterpretation, directly addressing somatoform disorders and the causation of mental disorders.

Brain function and consciousness, the development of neuroscience from phrenology to brain imaging, and implications for mental illness address physiological theories of personality and both major categories and causation of mental disorders.

Neuroscience covers the localisation of brain functions, consciousness, the neuroscience of emotion and decision-making, and the explanatory gap, addressing physiological theories of personality and the causation of mental disorders.

The Frankfurt School's synthesis of Marxist social theory with Freudian psychoanalysis, the analysis of authoritarianism and the authoritarian personality, and the critique of culture industry address psychoanalytic and social analytic theories of personality and environmental strains on personality functioning.

The concept of the individual traces development of selfhood, autonomy, and personal identity from Renaissance humanism through modern individualism, directly addressing the development of awareness of self.

Consciousness addresses subjective experience, the relationship between brain states and mental experience, and implications for personal identity and free will, relevant to the subjective aspect of personality and the causation of mental disorders.

Consciousness, neural correlates of awareness, altered states of consciousness, and the explanatory gap address the subjective development of awareness of self and neurological factors in mental disorders.

The four humours theory directly addresses physiological theories of personality, with its association of humours with temperaments (sanguine, phlegmatic, choleric, melancholic) and its lasting influence on personality theory.

Mrs Dalloway's parallel narrative of shell-shocked veteran Septimus Smith and its engagement with mental illness directly illustrate major categories of mental disorders including post-traumatic stress.
